old_claim.note: Philosophy provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency is retained because libraries can be organized collections of legal, administrative, literary, divinatory, or scientific texts without depending on philosophy as a technology.

source_shape.source_locator: What is the Library of Ashurbanipal section describes over 30,000 cuneiform tablets from Nineveh under Ashurbanipal, who ruled 669-c.631 BCE.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The British Museum anchors the library to cuneiform tablet collection and palace archive practice rather than to Greek philosophical schools.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Philosophy can be library content, but it is not a prerequisite for library institutions; requiring it is a content-domain dependency, not an operational technology dependency.

ontology_before: The library node treated philosophy as an enabling predecessor, implying philosophical traditions were required for library institutions.
ontology_after: The library node remains anchored to writing and no longer treats one content tradition as an operational prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant changed: Libraries must not directly depend on philosophy unless a narrower philosophical-library node is introduced.
would_reject_if: A source shows this Libraries node is intentionally scoped only to philosophical collections.
The graph splits archives from libraries and keeps this node only for institutions built around philosophical schools.
A source demonstrates that the Ashurbanipal collection should not be treated as a library-like institution.
short_reason: Libraries can organize many kinds of written records and do not require philosophy as a prerequisite.
